title: The 2003 US-led invasion of Iraq
summary: In the early hours of 20 March 2003, a coalition led by the United States and the United Kingdom, with contingents from Australia and Poland, opened Operation Iraqi Freedom with a targeted cruise-missile strike on a compound in southern Baghdad believed to house Saddam Hussein, followed within hours by a broader 'shock and awe' air campaign and a ground push across the Kuwaiti border. The stated goal was the disarmament of Iraqi weapons of mass destruction and the removal of Saddam's Ba'athist government. No such weapons were ever found. Baghdad fell three weeks later on 9 April, images of the toppling of Saddam's statue in Firdos Square broadcast around the world. What followed was not the swift stabilisation promised in Washington but an insurgency, a sectarian civil war, and, ultimately, the emergence of the Islamic State. Recorded Iraqi civilian deaths documented by Iraq Body Count exceeded 100,000 by the time of the American withdrawal in December 2011; higher survey estimates run into the hundreds of thousands. The war reshaped Iraq, unsettled the wider Middle East, exposed the limits of American power, and permanently damaged the credibility of Western intelligence services.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: President George W. Bush, whose administration made the case for war on the basis of alleged Iraqi weapons of mass destruction and ties to al-Qaeda.

sections.body:
The road from the terrorist attacks of 11 September 2001 to the invasion of Iraq eighteen months later was in retrospect remarkably short. Within days of the attacks on New York and Washington, senior officials in the administration of George W. Bush, including Deputy Defense Secretary Paul Wolfowitz and Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld, were arguing that Iraq should be a target of the American response, alongside al-Qaeda's actual sponsors in Afghanistan. Bush himself, in his State of the Union address on 29 January 2002, grouped Iraq with Iran and North Korea in an 'axis of evil' whose pursuit of weapons of mass destruction threatened the world.

Saddam Hussein had ruled Iraq since 1979. His invasion of Kuwait in August 1990 had provoked the first Gulf War and a decade of United Nations sanctions, weapons inspections, and no-fly zones enforced by American and British aircraft. Under the terms of Security Council Resolution 687 of April 1991 he was required to destroy his chemical, biological, and nuclear-weapons programmes and to accept intrusive inspections by the UN Special Commission (UNSCOM) and the International Atomic Energy Agency. By 1998 UNSCOM had been withdrawn amid disputes over Iraqi obstruction, and the inspection regime was suspended. In November 2002 the Security Council unanimously adopted Resolution 1441, giving Iraq 'a final opportunity to comply with its disarmament obligations' and returning inspectors under the new UN Monitoring, Verification and Inspection Commission (UNMOVIC) led by the Swedish diplomat Hans Blix.

Through the winter of 2002-2003, Blix's team conducted more than seven hundred inspections across Iraq and reported that they were finding neither active weapons programmes nor the smoking-gun evidence Washington had promised. On 5 February 2003 the American Secretary of State, Colin Powell, presented to the UN Security Council what he called 'evidence' of Iraqi chemical and biological weapons, mobile laboratories, and links to al-Qaeda, using satellite images, communications intercepts, and diagrams. Much of the material he presented was later shown to be false or wildly overstated. France, Germany, Russia, and China opposed the immediate use of force and pressed for continued inspections. Millions marched against war in London, Rome, Barcelona, Berlin, and New York on 15 February 2003, in what remains one of the largest coordinated global protests in history. Unable to obtain a second Security Council resolution explicitly authorising war, Bush and British Prime Minister Tony Blair proceeded on the basis of a legal argument, still contested, that existing resolutions provided sufficient authority.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: U.S. Marines from the 2nd Battalion, 1st Marine Regiment escort Iraqi prisoners of war on 21 March 2003, the second day of the ground invasion.
image.credit: Lance Cpl. Brian L. Wickliffe, U.S. Marine Corps

sections.body:
The war began at approximately 05:34 Baghdad time on 20 March 2003 with a strike by Tomahawk cruise missiles and F-117 stealth aircraft on the Dora Farms compound south of the capital, where intelligence had briefly suggested Saddam and his sons were sleeping. The strike missed. Hours later the main air campaign, popularly known as 'shock and awe', opened with the co-ordinated bombardment of command-and-control targets across Iraq. A ground force of some 148,000 American, 46,000 British, 2,000 Australian, and 194 Polish troops, under the overall command of General Tommy Franks at U.S. Central Command in Tampa, crossed the Kuwaiti border and advanced up two axes toward Baghdad, with the U.S. 3rd Infantry Division and 1st Marine Expeditionary Force in the lead and the British 1st Armoured Division peeling off toward Basra.

Organised Iraqi resistance was uneven. Conventional units of the regular army collapsed or dispersed after brief engagements; the Fedayeen Saddam paramilitaries and Republican Guard divisions fought harder in cities such as Nasiriyah, where the U.S. Marines took heavy casualties in late March, and around Karbala, where the 3rd Infantry Division punched through the so-called Karbala Gap on 2 April. A sandstorm from 24 to 26 March slowed the advance and gave rise to premature reports of a stalled campaign. On 4 April American armour reached Baghdad International Airport; on 5 and 7 April the 3rd Infantry Division conducted the two 'Thunder Runs' straight into central Baghdad. On 9 April, in a moment beamed live around the world, U.S. Marines of Task Force 2-4 helped a small crowd of Iraqis and journalists in Firdos Square topple a bronze statue of Saddam Hussein with an M88 recovery vehicle and a chain. Saddam himself had gone into hiding.

British forces entered Basra on 6 April after a two-week siege. Kurdish peshmerga forces, backed by U.S. special forces and airpower, took Kirkuk on 10 April and Mosul on 11 April. On 1 May, standing beneath a banner reading 'Mission Accomplished' on the deck of the USS Abraham Lincoln off the California coast, President Bush declared the end of 'major combat operations' in Iraq. Iraqi civilian deaths during the invasion phase are estimated at 3,200 to 7,400. Coalition combat deaths totalled 172 (139 American, 33 British). Iraqi military dead were estimated by Western analysts at 11,000 to 45,000. On 13 December 2003 Saddam Hussein was captured by U.S. forces in a spider-hole near his birthplace of Tikrit; he was tried by an Iraqi tribunal, convicted of crimes against humanity for the killing of 148 Shia villagers at Dujail in 1982, and hanged on 30 December 2006.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The occupation that followed proved catastrophic. Two orders issued in May 2003 by L. Paul Bremer, head of the Coalition Provisional Authority, would shape the next decade: CPA Order Number 1 formally banned tens of thousands of members of Saddam's Ba'ath Party from public employment, and CPA Order Number 2 disbanded the Iraqi army, sending some 400,000 armed and unemployed men home. Both orders were opposed by senior American military commanders on the ground and by parts of the State Department. Together they created the recruiting pool from which a Sunni insurgency, and later the Islamic State, would draw for years to come.

By the summer of 2003 a low-level insurgency was already active in the Sunni provinces of central and western Iraq. In August the UN headquarters in Baghdad was destroyed by a suicide truck bomb that killed the UN Special Representative Sergio Vieira de Mello and twenty-one others. In February 2006 the bombing of the al-Askari shrine in Samarra, one of the holiest sites in Shia Islam, triggered a full-scale sectarian civil war between Sunni and Shia militias that at its peak in 2006-2007 killed thousands of Iraqi civilians every month. In April 2004 photographs from Abu Ghraib prison, showing American military police abusing Iraqi detainees, destroyed what remained of the United States' moral case for the war. In 2007 Bush announced a 'surge' of some 30,000 additional troops, coupled with the tribal 'Awakening' movement in Anbar province and a shift in counterinsurgency doctrine under General David Petraeus, which reduced violence but did not resolve Iraq's political fractures.

The last American combat brigade withdrew in December 2011 under an agreement signed by the Bush administration and honoured by his successor Barack Obama. Iraq Body Count, a British-based project that counts only documented violent civilian deaths, recorded some 110,000 Iraqi civilian dead by that date. A 2006 Lancet study using cluster-sample survey methods put the total war-related death toll through mid-2006 at approximately 655,000; a 2013 study in PLOS Medicine estimated around half a million excess deaths through 2011. Sunni political marginalisation under Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki, the collapse of the Syrian state after 2011, and the withdrawal of American troops all fed the meteoric 2013-2014 rise of the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant, which in June 2014 captured Mosul and declared a caliphate, prompting a second American military intervention that lasted through 2017.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Iraq War transformed the strategic map of the Middle East, largely to the benefit of Iran. The removal of Saddam's Sunni-dominated Ba'athist state eliminated the main regional counterweight to the Islamic Republic and empowered Shia political parties, many of them with close ties to Tehran, that came to dominate the post-2003 Iraqi government. Iranian-linked militias operated openly on Iraqi soil for the next two decades. The war also contributed, indirectly, to the destabilisation of Syria after 2011, to the refugee crisis that flowed into Europe in 2015, and to the wider erosion of state authority across the region.

In Britain and the United States the war provoked years of political reckoning. The Iraq Inquiry chaired by Sir John Chilcot, which reported in July 2016 after seven years of work, concluded that the British government had joined the invasion 'before the peaceful options for disarmament had been exhausted', that the case for war had been presented 'with a certainty that was not justified', and that planning for the aftermath had been 'wholly inadequate'. In the United States a series of official reviews, including the Senate Intelligence Committee's 2004 and 2008 reports, documented the systemic failures of the pre-war intelligence process. No weapons of mass destruction were ever recovered. The Iraq Survey Group's final report, published under Charles Duelfer in September 2004, concluded that Saddam had destroyed his stockpiles in the mid-1990s but had wished to retain the option to reconstitute them once sanctions were lifted.

For Iraqis the legacy is measured in the more than one hundred thousand documented dead, the millions displaced internally or across borders, the destruction of much of the ancient urban fabric of Mosul in the anti-Islamic State campaign of 2016-2017, and a fragile, contested political order still shaped by 2003. For the wider world the war marked the end of the brief post-Cold War moment in which American military primacy seemed uncontested. The idea that the United States could remake the domestic politics of a large, complex Middle Eastern country by force did not survive Iraq.

figures.name: George W. Bush
figures.role: 43rd President of the United States; ordered the invasion and declared the end of 'major combat operations' aboard the USS Abraham Lincoln on 1 May 2003
figures.credit: Eric Draper / White House, Public domain

figures.name: Tony Blair
figures.role: British Prime Minister who committed the United Kingdom to the invasion; the 2016 Chilcot Inquiry concluded the decision was taken before peaceful options were exhausted
figures.credit: Paul Morse / White House, Public domain

figures.name: Saddam Hussein
figures.role: President of Iraq from 1979; captured by U.S. forces near Tikrit in December 2003 and executed after conviction by an Iraqi tribunal in December 2006
figures.credit: Iraqi Ministry of Information / Public domain

figures.name: Colin Powell
figures.role: U.S. Secretary of State who presented the case for war to the UN Security Council on 5 February 2003; later described the speech as a 'blot' on his record

figures.name: Hans Blix
figures.role: Swedish diplomat and head of UNMOVIC, whose weapons inspectors reported through the winter of 2002-2003 that they were finding no evidence of active Iraqi WMD programmes

images.caption: The bronze statue of Saddam Hussein in Baghdad's Firdos Square is pulled down on 9 April 2003, one of the most-broadcast images of the war.
An M1A1 Abrams tank of the U.S. Army beneath the Hands of Victory arch in central Baghdad, November 2003, seven months after the fall of the city.
images.credit: TSgt John L. Houghton Jr., U.S. Air Force
